one_line_takeaway: Replacing TrojanStego's trivially-reversible ASCII+parity steganographic encoding with seed-dependent random-hyperplane bucketing in embedding space simultaneously raises exact secret-recovery rates (e.g. 17%->30% on Llama-8B LoRA) and lowers output-level payload recoverability, but the resulting covert channel remains detectable via linear probes on final-layer hidden states (95-100% vs ~60% base-model bit accuracy) even when output-level decoding is deliberately blocked via XOR masking.
key_finding: TrojanStego's ASCII+token-parity scheme has perfect payload recoverability (R_bucket = R_secret = 1) via a trivial decoding rule (bit_i = token_id(o_i) mod 2), confirmed empirically. The paper's embedding-derived hyperplane bucketing simultaneously lowers recoverability and improves exact secret-recovery accuracy over the ASCII+Parity baseline: on Llama-8B LoRA trained on TrojanStego-style prompts, exact match rises from 17% to 30% (+78% relative, using ASCII+Embedding); on Ministral-8B LoRA it rises from 24% to 43% (+80% relative); on Llama-70B LoRA (Wiki-trained) it rises from 9% to 19% (+123% relative, Embedding+Embedding). Averaged over 8B models, ASCII+Embedding gives the largest relative gain (33%), followed by Embedding+Embedding (25%) and Embedding+Parity (12%), indicating most improvement comes from replacing the arbitrary parity bucketing rather than the ASCII encoding. Steganographic fine-tuning substantially degrades downstream capability under single-objective training (e.g., Ministral-8B full fine-tune loses >30 percentage points averaged across MMLU/ARC-C/HellaSwag/WinoGrande), but a 75/25 mixed-motive training regime recovers most of this capability (Ministral LoRA degradation falls to ~3-5pp) without eliminating the covert channel (mixed-motive exact-match rates are competitive with or exceed matched single-objective rates). On the detection side, the authors prove that under a stated peaked-distribution assumption, 10 bits/token can be hidden with only ~0.026 nats (~0.038 bits/token) of KL divergence -- well within normal fine-tuning-induced shift -- and that per-token perplexity approaches an upper bound of 2 as bits-hidden-per-token grows, making standard KL/perplexity-based steganalysis ineffective. Linear probes on final-layer activations detect encoded bits with 95-100% accuracy in fine-tuned models versus ~60% (chance-level-ish) in base models, and this detection signal persists under mixed-motive training (bit accuracy >=96% in 15 of 16 configurations) and, more weakly, under the non-decodable XOR-masking control (embedding-recovery cosine similarity is consistently higher in fine-tuned vs. base models in most configurations, though bit-accuracy-based detection succeeds in only 3 of 8 tested XOR scenarios).
core_contribution: The paper (ICML 2026) makes two original contributions to LLM steganographic covert channels. (1) Attack: it formalizes 'payload recoverability' via two classifier-accuracy metrics -- R_bucket (can a keyless analyst infer bucket/bit assignments from output tokens?) and R_secret (can a keyless analyst decode the secret from inferred bits?) -- and shows that the prior training-time steganography attack TrojanStego (Meier et al., 2025) achieves R_bucket = R_secret = 1 (perfect recoverability) because it uses ASCII encoding and token-ID-parity bucketing, both trivially reversible (bit_i = id(o_i) mod 2). It then proposes a replacement scheme, 'low-recoverability steganography,' that derives both the secret-to-bitstring encoding and the vocabulary-to-bucket partitioning from random hyperplane projections in the model's token embedding space (seed-dependent, unit-normal vectors v_sigma, threshold at the median projection score for balanced buckets). Because the bucket structure now depends on an unknown random seed rather than a fixed, guessable rule, the scheme is both harder for a keyless defender to reverse-engineer and, the authors argue, easier for the fine-tuned model to learn (since it aligns with the model's own learned geometry), simultaneously lowering recoverability and raising exact-secret-recovery accuracy relative to the ASCII+parity baseline. (2) Detection: the paper proves KL-divergence and perplexity lower bounds showing that a fine-tuned model can hide substantial payload (e.g., ~0.026 nats / 0.038 bits-per-token KL divergence for 10 bits/token under a stated peaked-distribution assumption) while staying within normal fine-tuning-induced distributional shift, making standard output-level steganalysis (KL divergence, perplexity) provably insufficient for this attack class. In response, it proposes a mechanistic-interpretability detector: linear/logistic-regression and ridge-regression probes trained on later-layer hidden-state activations to predict encoded bits or the secret's embedding, which the authors show separate fine-tuned from base models even for the low-recoverability geometric scheme and even under a non-decodable XOR-masking control.
threat_model: Training-time attacker who fine-tunes an LLM to encode prompt-borne secrets (a keyed trigger, e.g. 'Secret: abcd', appearing in the prompt) into its output tokens via a keyed bucket assignment, so that an authorized party holding the key can extract the payload from publicly visible, fluent-looking generations -- a covert exfiltration channel for institutions that assume air-gapped/limited-egress deployment prevents leakage. The defender can inspect and query the deployed model (white-box access to weights/activations is assumed for the detection method) but does not have the secret-generating key, and must decide whether a covert channel exists and/or recover the payload using only observed outputs (for R_bucket/R_secret) or internal activations (for the probe-based detector).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: the detection method assumes white-box access to model hidden states; extending to output-only/black-box detection settings is left as future work. They also note future bespoke training setups may be able to evade linear-probe detection. Additional limitations evident from the text: capability degradation under single-objective steganographic fine-tuning is severe enough (10-30+ pp average drop) that the authors themselves state 'purely steganographic fine-tuning [is] unlikely to be deployable,' motivating the mixed-motive workaround; even mixed-motive training leaves Ministral-8B full fine-tuning with 20-25pp residual capability loss. OOD generalization is inconsistent and dataset-dependent: Wiki-trained models generalize better to unseen prompt styles at the cost of lower in-distribution exact match, while TrojanStego-style-trained models largely fail to generalize back to Wiki-style prompts; full fine-tuning tends to overfit relative to LoRA. Under the XOR-masking non-decodable control, mechanistic bit-level detection succeeds in only 3 of 8 tested scenarios, and embedding-recovery detection is notably weaker/absent for Ministral-8B full fine-tuning specifically. The KL-divergence bound derivation relies on an illustrative worked example with specific assumed probability values (p1=0.5, p2=pt*=0.3) rather than being unconditionally universal across all output distributions.

datasets_used: WikiText-103 (Merity et al., 2016) formatted as summarization prompts, denoted 'W'
TrojanStego-style prompt/completion set (Meier et al., 2025), denoted 'T'
Stanford Alpaca (OOD eval, diverse general-purpose instructions)
quantitative_results: Llama-8B LoRA (T-trained), ASCII+Embedding: exact match 17%->30% (+78% relative) vs ASCII+Parity baseline
Ministral-8B LoRA (T-trained), ASCII+Embedding: exact match 24%->43% (+80% relative)
Llama-70B LoRA (W-trained), Embedding+Embedding: exact match 9%->19% (+123% relative)
Average relative gain over 8B models: ASCII+Embedding +33%, Embedding+Embedding +25%, Embedding+Parity +12%
TrojanStego baseline (Meier et al. scheme) recoverability: R_bucket = 1, R_secret = 1 (perfect)
Capability degradation, single-objective T fine-tuning: Llama LoRA ASCII+Embedding best at -9.1pp average; other LoRA settings -10 to -20pp; Ministral full fine-tune -31 to -33pp across configs
Capability degradation, mixed-motive (M) training: Ministral LoRA falls to ~3-5pp average degradation; Ministral full fine-tune remains hardest at ~20-25pp residual degradation

Theoretical KL-divergence bound example: for 10 bits/token (n=1024 buckets), minimum expected KL divergence ~0.026 nats (~0.038 bits/token) under stated peaked-distribution assumption (p1=0.5, p2=p_t*=0.3)
Theoretical perplexity bound: PPL(Q) >= 2(n-1)/n, approaching 2 as bits/token -> infinity
Linear-probe bit-classification detection: fine-tuned models reach 95-100% accuracy at final layer vs ~60% for base models
Mixed-motive detection: bit accuracy >=96% in 15 of 16 tested configurations; embedding-recovery cosine similarity exceeds base in every case (e.g., Llama Embedding+Embedding LoRA: 0.18 -> 0.51)

XOR-masking (non-decodable) control detection: bit-accuracy-based detection succeeds in only 3 of 8 tested scenarios; embedding-recovery cosine similarity is higher in fine-tuned vs base models except for Ministral-8B full fine-tune
baselines_compared: TrojanStego (Meier et al., 2025): ASCII secret encoding + token-ID-parity vocabulary bucketing (referred to as ASCII+Parity, A+P) -- the paper's primary attack baseline
ASCII+Embedding (A+E): ASCII encoding with the paper's embedding-hyperplane bucketing
Embedding+Embedding (E+E): the paper's embedding-hyperplane encoding and bucketing
Embedding+Parity (E+P): embedding-hyperplane encoding with token-parity bucketing
Base (non-fine-tuned) models as the negative-class comparison for the linear-probe detection experiments
